En:
This voiced region exhibits harmonic degradation in the mid-frequency band during the

consonant "v"

. The visual evidence shows smeared harmonic stacks instead of clean, periodic patterns, which directly translates to a metallic-sounding speech artifact where the natural harmonic structure is disrupted.

This voiced region shows harmonic degradation in the low-frequency band. Instead of the sharp transient expected from a

consonant

, the pattern looks smeared and grainy. This weakens the clean structure of the sound and gives it a slightly metallic quality.

This voiced region in the high-frequency band shows harmonic degradation, where the

vowel

's harmonic structure appears smeared rather than cleanly stacked. This gives the vowel a metallic quality and makes it sound less natural than genuine speech.

In this speech region, the

consonant

segment within the mid-frequency band exhibits noise flattening, with the spectral fluctuations expected in genuine speech replaced by an unnaturally smooth noise layer. This results in an airless, hollow consonant quality that lacks the organic texture of authentic speech.

This voiced region shows formant fading in the high-frequency

vowel

range, where genuine speech would normally retain clear formant structure. The weakening of formant bands above 3.5 kHz gives the vowel a hollow quality, reducing the natural resonance expected in authentic speech.

This speech region in the mid-frequency band shows harmonic degradation, where the sharp transient burst expected for the

"t" consonant

is replaced by a smeared, grainy spread of energy. This artifact gives the consonant a metallic quality.

This low-frequency region shows noise flattening in

unvoiced

segments, visible as a uniform sheet of noise with little spectral speckle. This smoothing suppresses the natural variability of vocal tract noise, producing an airless quality that reduces the sense of genuineness in the speech.

This speech region shows periodic texture, with low-frequency patterns duplicated into the high-frequency band instead of the irregular energy bursts expected in a genuine

fricative

. This gives the speech a robotic quality.

This

unvoiced

region in the low-frequency band shows noise flattening, where the absence of granular spectral speckle creates an unnaturally uniform noise profile. This gives the sibilance a hollow quality and removes the organic background noise expected in natural speech.

In this <F>mid-frequency</F> <T>non-speech</T> region, noise flattening appears as a uniform noise layer with reduced spectral speckle. The resulting airless quality departs from the textured variability of authentic audio.
